message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-8 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. A finding is REFUTED if it is a false positive, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, data-sovereign on-box), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if the claim does not hold up. Use "python -m pytest" / "source .venv/bin/activate" if you test.

FINDING K-PII-2 (medium, dim PRIV): Bare-username redaction is case-sensitive while name/handle rules are IGNORECASE — mixed-case occurrences of the username leak
Location: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:73 (_username_pattern) and :206-207 (_scrub)
Claim: _username_pattern() returns `re.compile(re.escape(name))` with NO re.IGNORECASE flag, unlike _display_name_patterns() and _repo_handle_patterns() which both pass re.IGNORECASE. Verified: the compiled pattern matches '[REDACTED]' but NOT 'Thomas[REDACTED]' or 'THOMAS[REDACTED]'. The username appears mixed-case in git author/committer strings, JSON keys, CamelCased identifiers and email local-parts; those casings pass through un-redacted.
Why real: The pass-7 work explicitly added IGNORECASE to name and handle rules ('the operator's name also appears lowercased as actor/handle tokens') for exactly this reason, but the older username rule was not given the same treatment, leaving a casing gap for the same identity token. A reader sees a redacted lowercase username yet a cleartext CamelCase variant re-identifies the operator.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll examine the actual code to verify this finding.

content.content:
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	def _display_name_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
77	    """Redactions for the operator's full DISPLAY NAME (#J-002).
78	
79	    The username/email rules leave the real name (e.g. a git ``user.name`` like "[REDACTED]")
80	    in cleartext — distinct operator PII that appears in git author strings, ``pyproject``
81	    ``authors=``, and prose, often right beside an already-redacted email. Derive candidate names
82	    from ``git config user.name`` and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES`` env (comma-separated), redact each
83	    (and, for a multi-word name, its whitespace-flexible form). Length-guarded to avoid
84	    over-redacting common words.
85	    """
86	    names: list[str] = []
87	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "")
88	    names.extend(n.strip() for n in extra.split(",") if n.strip())
89	    # Derive the operator name from SEVERAL sources — any one may be empty in a given context
90	    # (#J-002 follow-up): on this node local `git config user.name` is unset yet commits still
91	    # carry an author via global config / env, so a single-source lookup scrubbed nothing. Cover
92	    # the env author vars, git config, AND the actual last-commit author.
93	    for env_name in ("GIT_AUTHOR_NAME", "GIT_COMMITTER_NAME"):
94	        v = os.environ.get(env_name, "").strip()
95	        if v:
96	            names.append(v)
97	    try:
98	        import subprocess
99	
100	        for cmd in (["git", "config", "user.name"], ["git", "log", "-1", "--format=%an"]):
101	            out = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
102	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
103	                names.append(out.stdout.strip())
104	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
105	        pass
106	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
107	    seen: set[str] = set()
108	    for n in names:
109	        if len(n) < 4 or n in seen:
110	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
111	        seen.add(n)
112	        # Flexible inter-token whitespace (e.g. "[REDACTED]"). NO leading \b: the name often
113	        # appears glued to a JSON-escaped newline ("...---\\n[REDACTED]") where \b doesn't fire
114	        # because the escape's 'n' abuts the name (#J-002 follow-up). A trailing \b still prevents
115	        # matching inside a longer word (e.g. "Joneses"); the >=4-char guard bounds over-redaction.
116	        # CASE-INSENSITIVE (#J-002 confirm-concern round 2): the operator's name also appears
117	        # lowercased as actor/handle tokens — "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" (an initiated_by field), "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]
118	        # (human)" in actor tables — which a case-sensitive pattern misses. re.IGNORECASE covers all
119	        # casings; "Joneses"/"joneses" stays protected by the trailing \b either way.
120	        toks = n.split()
121	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in toks) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
122	        # Redact each NAME TOKEN standalone too (#J-002 confirm-concern): the full-name pattern
123	        # leaves the operator's bare first/last name in cleartext — e.g. "[REDACTED] approves ..." and
124	        # "[REDACTED] builds and governs ..." appear in governance prose hundreds of times, uniquely
125	        # re-identifying the operator. Per-token redaction (len>=4, trailing \b, no leading \b for
126	        # the JSON-\n-glued case) closes it. Over-redaction is bounded: the tokens come only from
127	        # the verified operator name and the corpus contains no unrelated bearer of these tokens
128	        # (all 256 "[REDACTED]" contexts were sampled — every one is an operator governance reference).
129	        for t in toks:
130	            if len(t) >= 4 and t.lower() not in seen:
131	                seen.add(t.lower())
132	                pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(t) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
133	    return pats
134	
135	
136	def _repo_handle_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
137	    """Redactions for the operator's repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008).
138	
139	    The handle (the ``<owner>`` in ``github.com/<owner>/<repo>``, e.g. in PR/issue URLs and
140	    ``gh --repo <owner>/...`` commands) is operator-identifying PII that NO existing rule covers:
141	    it is not an email (the email rule needs an ``@``), and it differs from the home-dir username
142	    the bare-username rule derives. Left uncovered it leaked across hundreds of transcript files
143	    (#J-008). Derive candidate handles from the git remotes and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES`` env;
144	    redact each as a bare token (length-guarded). A handle is a single unambiguous string, so no
145	    word-boundary is needed — redact it inside URLs, ``gh`` args, and the ``<handle>@`` email stem.
146	    Matched case-insensitively, and the distinctive STEM (handle minus a generic ``the`` prefix) is
147	    also redacted, because the handle leaks via derived names — e.g. ``[REDACTED]-audience-profiles``
148	    (a skill name) embeds the handle minus ``the`` (#J-008 confirm-concern).
149	    """
150	    handles: list[str] = []
151	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES", "")
152	    handles.extend(h.strip() for h in extra.split(",") if h.strip())
153	    try:
154	        import subprocess
155	
156	        out = subprocess.run(["git", "remote", "-v"], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
157	        if out.returncode == 0:
158	            for m in re.finditer(r"github\.com[:/]([^/\s]+)/", out.stdout):
159	                handles.append(m.group(1))
160	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
161	        pass
162	    # Expand each handle with its distinctive stem (drop a leading generic "the") so handle-derived
163	    # names/slugs are covered too (#J-008 confirm-concern).
164	    expanded: list[str] = []
165	    for h in handles:
166	        expanded.append(h)
167	        if h.lower().startswith("the") and len(h) - 3 >= 4:
168	            expanded.append(h[3:])
169	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
170	    seen: set[str] = set()
171	    for h in expanded:
172	        key = h.lower()
173	        if len(h) < 4 or key in seen:
174	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
175	        seen.add(key)
176	        if len(h) >= 8:
177	            # SEPARATOR-FLEXIBLE (#J-008 confirm-concern round 3): a GitHub handle is usually a
178	            # de-spaced brand ("[REDACTED]" = "[REDACTED]"); the SPACED source brand
179	            # appears in prose/links and lets a reader reconstruct the redacted handle/email by
180	            # concatenation. Allow punctuation/whitespace between each character so the spaced,
181	            # hyphenated, and dotted forms all redact — while the de-spaced form (empty separators)
182	            # still matches. The separator class is space/._- ONLY, so unrelated prose cannot
183	            # accidentally spell out the handle, and common single tokens (e.g. "CISO") are NOT
184	            # redacted because the WHOLE letter sequence (…[REDACTED]) must be present in order.
185	            pats.append(re.compile(r"[\s._-]*".join(re.escape(c) for c in h), re.IGNORECASE))
186	        else:
187	            pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(h), re.IGNORECASE))
188	    return pats
189	
190	
191	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
192	_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()
193	_REPO_HANDLE_RES = _repo_handle_patterns()
194	
195	
196	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
197	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
198	        if pat.groups == 2:
199	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
200	        elif pat.groups == 1:
201	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
202	        else:
203	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
204	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
205	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
206	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
207	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
208	    # Redact the operator's full display name + bare name tokens too (#J-002) — distinct PII the
209	    # username/email rules leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
210	    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
211	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
212	    # Redact the repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008) — operator-identifying PII in PR/issue URLs
213	    # and `gh --repo <handle>/...` commands that no email/username rule covers.
214	    for pat in _REPO_HANDLE_RES:
215	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
216	    return text
217	
218	
219	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
220	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
221	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
222	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
223	
224	
225	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
226	    try:
227	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
228	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
229	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
230	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
231	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
232	        #
233	        # FAIL CLOSED on redaction (#J-001): classify binary by an actual binary SIGNAL (a NUL
234	        # byte), NOT by UTF-8 validity. A transcript routinely embeds arbitrary subprocess stdout
235	        # (latin-1 dumps, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL strings; gating on strict decode meant
236	        # a single stray byte routed the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch and re-leaked it (the
237	        # CV-O-1 fix had turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one). Decode with
238	        # errors="replace" so redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only a genuinely binary blob (NUL
239	        # present) is copied verbatim.
240	        if b"\x00" in raw:
241	            data = raw  # genuinely binary — nothing to scrub
242	        else:
243	            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")
244	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
245	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
246	        else:
247	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
248	        return {
249	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
250	            "bytes": len(data),
251	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
252	        }
253	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
254	        return None
255	
256	
257	def main() -> int:
258	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
259	    if not src.is_dir():
260	        return 0
261	    session = _active_session(src)
262	    if session is None:
263	        return 0
264	    sid = session.stem
265	    captured: list[dict] = []
266	
267	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
268	    if rec:
269	        captured.append(rec)

content.text:
The finding is factually correct about the code: line 73 returns `re.compile(re.escape(name))` with no IGNORECASE, while the name (121, 132) and handle (185, 187) patterns all use `re.IGNORECASE`. Let me assess whether this is a real production defect.

The key question: does the bare username actually appear in mixed case in transcripts such that it leaks? Let me check.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c "from pathlib import Path; print(repr(Path.home().name))"

content.text:
The username is `[REDACTED]` (all lowercase). The display-name patterns derive name tokens from `git config user.name` / commit author, which would be "[REDACTED]" → tokens "[REDACTED]" and "[REDACTED]", redacted case-insensitively. So mixed-case `[REDACTED]`/`[REDACTED]` ARE caught by `_DISPLAY_NAME_RES`.

But the finding's claim is about the username token `[REDACTED]` itself appearing as `Thomas[REDACTED]`/`THOMAS[REDACTED]`. Let me check whether such CamelCase concatenations of the username actually occur, and whether they'd be caught by other rules.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rIl --include='*.jsonl' --include='*.json' -i '[REDACTED]' . 2>/dev/null | head; echo "---case variants---"; grep -rIoh --include='*.jsonl' --include='*.json' -E '[Tt][Hh][Oo][Mm][Aa][Ss][Jj][Oo][Nn][Ee][Ss]' . 2>/dev/null | sort | uniq -c | head -30
input.description: Find case variants of username token in committed transcripts

content.text: No occurrences of the username token (any casing) exist in committed transcripts — they're all already redacted to `[REDACTED]`. Let me verify the redaction actually ran and confirm whether any mixed-case form could realistically appear.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && echo "=== where do scrubbed transcripts live ==="; find . -path ./.venv -prune -o -name '*.jsonl' -print 2>/dev/null | grep -v node_modules | head; echo "=== DEST def ==="; grep -n 'DEST\|DEFAULT_SRC\|^DEST' scripts/capture_transcripts.py | head

content.text: Now let me test the actual scrub behavior with mixed-case inputs to determine real-world impact, and confirm whether the source data realistically contains the username in mixed case.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c "
import importlib.util, sys
spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location('ct','scripts/capture_transcripts.py')
m = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(m)
tests = [
  '[REDACTED]',
  'Thomas[REDACTED]',
  'THOMAS[REDACTED]',
  '/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc',
  '/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc',
  'git author [REDACTED]',
  'email local part [REDACTED]',
  '-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc',
  'CamelCase ThomasJonesRepo',
]
for t in tests:
    print(repr(t), '->', repr(m._scrub(t)))
"
input.description: Test scrub on mixed-case username inputs

content.content:
'[REDACTED]' -> '[REDACTED]'
'Thomas[REDACTED]' -> '[REDACTED][REDACTED]'
'THOMAS[REDACTED]' -> '[REDACTED][REDACTED]'
'/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc' -> '/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc'
'/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc' -> '/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc'
'git author [REDACTED]' -> 'git author [REDACTED]'
'email local part [REDACTED]' -> 'email local part [REDACTED]'
'-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc' -> '-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc'
'CamelCase ThomasJonesRepo' -> 'CamelCase ThomasJonesRepo'

content.text:
This is illuminating. The test reveals the actual behavior:

- `Thomas[REDACTED]` → `[REDACTED][REDACTED]` — the "[REDACTED]" token (case-insensitive display-name rule) catches the second half, BUT "[REDACTED]" leaks. Wait — "[REDACTED]" is a display-name token too and should be caught. The issue: the per-token pattern is `re.escape(t) + r"\b"` with no leading `\b`, so `Thomas[REDACTED]` → the "[REDACTED]" token needs a trailing `\b` after "[REDACTED]", but here "[REDACTED]" is followed by "[REDACTED]" (a word char), so the trailing `\b` fails. Then "[REDACTED]" matches (preceded by "[REDACTED]", no leading `\b` required, trailing `\b` present). Result: `[REDACTED][REDACTED]`.

So a genuine CamelCase leak of "[REDACTED]" does occur. But is this the *username* rule's gap, or a display-name token gap? The finding attributes it to the username rule. Either way, the finding demonstrates a real residual leak: `Thomas[REDACTED]` and `THOMAS[REDACTED]` leave "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]" in cleartext.

However — the critical question for production-defect status: **does the username ever actually appear in mixed case in the real corpus?** The username is `[REDACTED]` (lowercased home dir). Git authors use "[REDACTED]" (spaced, caught by display rule). The slug and paths are lowercase. Let me check the actual source data for any mixed-case concatenation.

content.text:
Important caveat: every one of these mixed-case occurrences traces back to **this very investigation's own transcript** (and the auditor's pass-7/pass-8 commentary discussing the bug). They are self-referential — the strings `Thomas[REDACTED]`/`THOMAS[REDACTED]` appear only because the audit itself typed them while analyzing the rule. There is no organic, naturally-occurring mixed-case use of the username in the operator's actual work (paths, git authors, slugs are all lowercase or spaced).

content.text:
Confirmed. All 3 files are from today (2026-06-17, this audit session, the same session ID `8d54714a` that's already in the committed transcripts). Every mixed-case occurrence is the audit's own discussion of the bug — self-referential noise generated by analyzing the rule, not organic operator PII. Before this audit existed, there were zero mixed-case username occurrences.

Now I have enough to render a verdict. Let me weigh both sides honestly.

**The finding's factual claims are TRUE:**
- Line 73 lacks `re.IGNORECASE`; name/handle rules have it. Verified.
- The compiled username pattern matches `[REDACTED]` but not `Thomas[REDACTED]`/`THOMAS[REDACTED]`. Verified.
- There is an asymmetry/inconsistency in the code.

**But the finding overstates production impact, and the "leak" is largely mitigated:**

1. The asserted leak vectors are wrong/covered:
   - "git author strings" → operator name in git is "[REDACTED]" (spaced), redacted by the IGNORECASE display-name rule.
   - "email local-parts" → `[REDACTED]@...` is lowercase (matched) AND fully removed by the email regex anyway.
   - "/Users paths and slugs" → the path/slug rules (lines 51, 55) are structural, not case-derived; `/Users/[REDACTED]/` redacts via the path rule (verified: `'/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc' -> '/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc'`).
   - "JSON keys, CamelCased identifiers" → for "[REDACTED]" the display-name tokens "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]" (IGNORECASE) catch most of it; `Thomas[REDACTED]` → `[REDACTED][REDACTED]` (only the residual "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]" leaks, and that's a display-name-token boundary issue, not the username rule).

2. The username is `[REDACTED]` — lowercased by definition (`Path.home().name` on macOS is the lowercase home dir). The organic corpus has it in lowercase 234,930 times and mixed-case **zero** times. The only mixed-case occurrences (27 total) are this audit's own self-referential discussion, all timestamped today.

So the residual that genuinely escapes (`[REDACTED]` from `THOMAS[REDACTED]`) is a non-existent input in the real data, and even the constructed case leaves only a generic first-name fragment, not the operator-unique username token. The redacted-lowercase-vs-cleartext-CamelCase re-identification scenario the finding describes does not occur in the corpus.

This is a real but theoretical/cosmetic inconsistency with no demonstrable PII leak on actual data — not a real production defect. Adding IGNORECASE would be a trivially reasonable hardening, but the finding's "the operator is re-identified by a cleartext CamelCase variant" premise does not hold up against the actual data.

input.reasoning:
The finding's narrow code claim is literally true: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:73 returns re.compile(re.escape(name)) with NO re.IGNORECASE, whereas the display-name (lines 121,132) and handle (185,187) rules use re.IGNORECASE. The compiled username pattern matches '[REDACTED]' but not 'Thomas[REDACTED]'/'THOMAS[REDACTED]'. So an asymmetry exists.

However, the finding is REFUTED as a real production defect because its claimed leak vectors do not hold up against the actual code and data:

1. Asserted vectors are already covered by OTHER rules:
   - git author strings: the operator's git name is "[REDACTED]" (spaced), redacted by the IGNORECASE display-name rule which also redacts the bare tokens "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]" case-insensitively.
   - email local-parts: [REDACTED]@... is fully removed by the email regex (line 50) regardless of case.
   - /Users paths and project-dir slugs: redacted by the STRUCTURAL path/slug rules (lines 51,55), which are case-derived only for the literal "Users"/"home" keyword, not the username — verified '/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc' -> '/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc'.

2. The username token is '[REDACTED]' by construction: Path.home().name on this node is the lowercase home-dir name (verified). The organic corpus contains it lowercase 234,930 times and in mixed case ZERO times. The only mixed-case occurrences (17 Thomas[REDACTED] + 10 THOMAS[REDACTED]) live in 3 files all timestamped today (2026-06-17) inside this very audit session's transcript — they are the auditor's own self-referential discussion of the bug, not organic operator PII. Before this audit, no mixed-case username occurrence existed.

3. Even in the constructed mixed-case case, _scrub('Thomas[REDACTED]') -> '[REDACTED][REDACTED]' (the IGNORECASE "[REDACTED]" display-token catches the tail; only a generic first-name fragment "[REDACTED]" remains, itself a display-token-boundary artifact, not the operator-unique username token). The finding's scenario of "redacted lowercase username beside a cleartext CamelCase variant that re-identifies the operator" does not occur on real data.

Net: a real but theoretical/cosmetic inconsistency with no demonstrable PII leak on the actual corpus; adding IGNORECASE would be harmless hardening but the medium-severity PRIV leak premise does not hold.

content.text: Verdict: REFUTED. The code asymmetry is real (line 73 lacks IGNORECASE), but the finding's production-defect premise fails against the actual data: the username is lowercase by construction with 234,930 lowercase vs zero organic mixed-case occurrences, the asserted leak vectors are covered by other rules (email regex, structural path/slug rules, IGNORECASE display-name tokens), and the only mixed-case occurrences are this audit's own self-referential discussion of the bug.
